content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the findings:

## QoS Classification Function Report

**Primary Function: `ComputePodQOS`**

**Implementations (3 locations):**

1. **pkg/apis/core/v1/helper/qos/qos.go** (lines 87-169)
   - Uses v1 API types (`v1.Pod`, `v1.PodQOSClass`)

2. **pkg/apis/core/helper/qos/qos.go** (lines 89-171)
   - Uses internal core types (`core.Pod`, `core.PodQOSClass`)
   - Note: Comment on line 88 says "When this function is updated please also update staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/util/qos/qos.go"

3. **staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/util/qos/qos.go** (lines 79-131)
   - Uses v1 API types (`core.Pod`, `core.PodQOSClass`)

**Core Decision Logic (lines 114-131 in kubectl version):**
```go
if len(requests) == 0 && len(limits) == 0 {
    return core.PodQOSBestEffort
}
if isGuaranteed {
    for name, req := range requests {
        if lim, exists := limits[name]; !exists || lim.Cmp(req) != 0 {
            isGuaranteed = false
            break
        }
    }
}
if isGuaranteed && len(requests) == len(limits) {
    return core.PodQOSGuaranteed
}
return core.PodQOSBurstable
```

**Resources Considered:**
- Only CPU and Memory (hardcoded: `supportedQoSComputeResources = sets.NewString(string(core.ResourceCPU), string(core.ResourceMemory))`)
- Non-compute resources are filtered out and ignored

**Container Handling:**
- Regular containers: **included** (lines 100-101: `pod.Spec.Containers...`)
- Init containers: **included** (lines 101: `pod.Spec.InitContainers...`)
- Ephemeral containers: **excluded** with explicit comment "ephemeral containers are not considered for QoS as they cannot define resources" (line 110)

**Function Pair:**
- `GetPodQOS(pod)` - Returns cached `.Status.QOSClass` if set; otherwise calls `ComputePodQOS`
- `ComputePodQOS(pod)` - Evaluates QoS by examining all container requests/limits

**Key Callers:**
- **Kubelet Eviction Manager** (`pkg/kubelet/eviction/eviction_manager.go:165`) — uses `GetPodQOS` to determine eviction priority
- **Pod Container Manager** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go:113`) — uses `GetPodQOS` for cgroup resource allocation
- **QoS Policy** (`pkg/kubelet/qos/policy.go:51`) — uses `GetPodQOS` to determine cgroup configuration

The classification is central to Kubernetes resource management: Guaranteed pods get hard resource limits, BestEffort pods are evicted first under pressure, and Burstable pods occupy the middle.

input.content:
# Pod QoS Classification — `ComputePodQOS`

## Context
The user asked which function in the Kubernetes source classifies a Pod into one of the three QoS classes (`Guaranteed`, `Burstable`, `BestEffort`) and what its decision rule is. This is an explanatory/discovery task — no code changes are intended.

## Answer

### Function

**`ComputePodQOS(pod) PodQOSClass`** is the canonical classifier. It is paired with **`GetPodQOS(pod)`**, which returns the cached value from `pod.Status.QOSClass` if present, otherwise falls through to `ComputePodQOS`.

The function exists in three near-identical copies (kept in sync by hand — see the comment on `pkg/apis/core/helper/qos/qos.go:88`):

| File | Lines | Types |
|---|---|---|
| `pkg/apis/core/v1/helper/qos/qos.go` | 87–169 | `v1.Pod`, `v1.PodQOSClass` |
| `pkg/apis/core/helper/qos/qos.go` | 89–171 | internal `core.Pod` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/util/qos/qos.go` | 79–131 | kubectl-side copy |

### Decision rule

The function walks every regular container and init container in the pod (ephemeral containers are explicitly excluded, see `qos.go:108`). For each container it sums positive CPU and memory `requests` and `limits` into two pod-level maps. Only CPU and memory count — `supportedQoSComputeResources` is hardcoded to those two (`qos.go:27`). Any other resource (e.g. `ephemeral-storage`, extended resources) is filtered out.

Per-container, it tracks whether *both* CPU and memory limits were set with a positive value; if any container is missing one of those limits, an `isGuaranteed` flag is cleared.

When the `PodLevelResources` feature gate is on and `pod.Spec.Resources` is set, the same logic runs once over the pod-level requests/limits instead of summing across containers (`qos.go:91–106`).

After the walk, the classification (lines 152–168):

1. **`BestEffort`** — both `requests` and `limits` maps are empty (no container set any positive CPU or memory request/limit).
2. **`Guaranteed`** — `isGuaranteed` is still true (every container set both CPU and memory limits) AND for every resource present in `requests`, the matching `limits` entry exists and is exactly equal (`lim.Cmp(req) == 0`) AND `len(requests) == len(limits)`. In practice this means: every container declares CPU and memory, with `request == limit` for both.
3. **`Burstable`** — anything in between: at least one positive request or limit exists, but the strict request==limit / all-resources-set rule failed.

### Why it matters / who calls it

`GetPodQOS` is consumed by:

- **Kubelet eviction manager** (`pkg/kubelet/eviction/eviction_manager.go:165`) — eviction order under node pressure.
- **Pod cgroup manager** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go:113`) — places the pod under the matching `kubepods/{guaranteed,burstable,besteffort}` cgroup.
- **QoS OOM policy** (`pkg/kubelet/qos/policy.go:51`) — sets `oom_score_adj` so BestEffort pods are killed first by the kernel OOM killer.

So the classification drives both Kubernetes-level eviction priority and Linux-level OOM priority.

## Files referenced (read-only)
- `pkg/apis/core/v1/helper/qos/qos.go`
- `pkg/apis/core/helper/qos/qos.go`
- `staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/util/qos/qos.go`
- `pkg/kubelet/eviction/eviction_manager.go`
- `pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go`
- `pkg/kubelet/qos/policy.go`

## Verification
No code changes are proposed; verification is reading the cited line ranges. To sanity-check the rule against real pods: `kubectl get pod <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.qosClass}'` returns the cached classification produced by this same logic during admission.

# Pod QoS Classification — `ComputePodQOS`

## Context
The user asked which function in the Kubernetes source classifies a Pod into one of the three QoS classes (`Guaranteed`, `Burstable`, `BestEffort`) and what its decision rule is. This is an explanatory/discovery task — no code changes are intended.

## Answer

### Function

**`ComputePodQOS(pod) PodQOSClass`** is the canonical classifier. It is paired with **`GetPodQOS(pod)`**, which returns the cached value from `pod.Status.QOSClass` if present, otherwise falls through to `ComputePodQOS`.

The function exists in three near-identical copies (kept in sync by hand — see the comment on `pkg/apis/core/helper/qos/qos.go:88`):

| File | Lines | Types |
|---|---|---|
| `pkg/apis/core/v1/helper/qos/qos.go` | 87–169 | `v1.Pod`, `v1.PodQOSClass` |
| `pkg/apis/core/helper/qos/qos.go` | 89–171 | internal `core.Pod` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/util/qos/qos.go` | 79–131 | kubectl-side copy |

### Decision rule

The function walks every regular container and init container in the pod (ephemeral containers are explicitly excluded, see `qos.go:108`). For each container it sums positive CPU and memory `requests` and `limits` into two pod-level maps. Only CPU and memory count — `supportedQoSComputeResources` is hardcoded to those two (`qos.go:27`). Any other resource (e.g. `ephemeral-storage`, extended resources) is filtered out.

Per-container, it tracks whether *both* CPU and memory limits were set with a positive value; if any container is missing one of those limits, an `isGuaranteed` flag is cleared.

When the `PodLevelResources` feature gate is on and `pod.Spec.Resources` is set, the same logic runs once over the pod-level requests/limits instead of summing across containers (`qos.go:91–106`).

After the walk, the classification (lines 152–168):

1. **`BestEffort`** — both `requests` and `limits` maps are empty (no container set any positive CPU or memory request/limit).
2. **`Guaranteed`** — `isGuaranteed` is still true (every container set both CPU and memory limits) AND for every resource present in `requests`, the matching `limits` entry exists and is exactly equal (`lim.Cmp(req) == 0`) AND `len(requests) == len(limits)`. In practice this means: every container declares CPU and memory, with `request == limit` for both.
3. **`Burstable`** — anything in between: at least one positive request or limit exists, but the strict request==limit / all-resources-set rule failed.

### Why it matters / who calls it

`GetPodQOS` is consumed by:

- **Kubelet eviction manager** (`pkg/kubelet/eviction/eviction_manager.go:165`) — eviction order under node pressure.
- **Pod cgroup manager** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go:113`) — places the pod under the matching `kubepods/{guaranteed,burstable,besteffort}` cgroup.
- **QoS OOM policy** (`pkg/kubelet/qos/policy.go:51`) — sets `oom_score_adj` so BestEffort pods are killed first by the kernel OOM killer.

So the classification drives both Kubernetes-level eviction priority and Linux-level OOM priority.

## Files referenced (read-only)
- `pkg/apis/core/v1/helper/qos/qos.go`
- `pkg/apis/core/helper/qos/qos.go`
- `staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/util/qos/qos.go`
- `pkg/kubelet/eviction/eviction_manager.go`
- `pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go`
- `pkg/kubelet/qos/policy.go`

## Verification
No code changes are proposed; verification is reading the cited line ranges. To sanity-check the rule against real pods: `kubectl get pod <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.qosClass}'` returns the cached classification produced by this same logic during admission.

**`ComputePodQOS(pod) PodQOSClass`** — paired with **`GetPodQOS(pod)`** which returns the cached `pod.Status.QOSClass` if set, else falls through to compute.

It lives in three near-identical copies, kept in sync by hand:

- `pkg/apis/core/v1/helper/qos/qos.go:87-169` (v1 API types — the one most callers use)
- `pkg/apis/core/helper/qos/qos.go:89-171` (internal core types)
- `staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/util/qos/qos.go:79-131` (kubectl copy)

## Decision rule

It walks every regular container plus every init container (ephemeral containers are explicitly excluded — `qos.go:108`) and sums positive **CPU and memory** requests/limits into two pod-level maps. Only CPU and memory count; `supportedQoSComputeResources` is hardcoded to those two at `qos.go:27`, so things like `ephemeral-storage` or extended resources are ignored.

Per container it also tracks whether *both* CPU and memory limits are set with a positive value; if any container misses one, an `isGuaranteed` flag is cleared.

(When the `PodLevelResources` feature gate is on and `pod.Spec.Resources` is set, the same logic runs once on the pod-level resources instead of summing across containers — `qos.go:91-106`.)

Then, lines 152-168:

1. **`BestEffort`** — both `requests` and `limits` maps are empty. No container set any positive CPU or memory request/limit.
2. **`Guaranteed`** — `isGuaranteed` is still true (every container set both CPU and memory limits) **and** for every resource in `requests` the matching `limits` entry exists and is exactly equal (`lim.Cmp(req) == 0`) **and** `len(requests) == len(limits)`. In practice: every container declares CPU and memory with `request == limit` for both.
3. **`Burstable`** — anything else: at least one positive request or limit exists, but the strict equality / both-resources-set rule failed.

## Why it matters

`GetPodQOS` is consumed by:

- Kubelet eviction manager (`pkg/kubelet/eviction/eviction_manager.go:165`) — eviction order under node pressure.
- Pod cgroup manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go:113`) — places the pod under `kubepods/{guaranteed,burstable,besteffort}`.
- QoS OOM policy (`pkg/kubelet/qos/policy.go:51`) — sets `oom_score_adj` so BestEffort dies first to the kernel OOM killer.

So the classification drives both Kubernetes-level eviction priority and Linux-level OOM priority.
